title: Spanish Chicken Paella

subs.name: Sona masoori or kolam rice are ideal; avoid basmati as the long grain stays separate rather than absorbing stock
subs.notes: Short to medium-grain varieties give the correct sticky-yet-distinct texture

ingredients.name: ripe tomatoes, grated or finely chopped
red capsicum (shimla mirch), sliced into strips

subs.name: Shimla mirch (same vegetable, different name) is universally available; the red variety adds sweetness and colour
A quarter teaspoon of turmeric dissolved in the soaking water gives similar colour without the floral flavour; Kashmiri saffron from Indian spice shops works perfectly
Sunflower oil or any neutral refined oil; olive oil adds a mild fruitiness but is not essential to the final flavour

ingredients.name: hot chicken stock (or stock cube dissolved in 750 ml water)

subs.name: Maggi or Knorr chicken stock cube dissolved in 750 ml hot water; or plain water with a bay leaf and an extra half teaspoon of salt

ingredients.name: paprika or Kashmiri red chilli powder
lemon, cut into wedges for serving

steps.text: Steep the saffron strands in 4 tablespoons of hot water for 10 minutes; the liquid will turn a rich amber-orange. Season the chicken pieces all over with salt and 1 teaspoon of the paprika, pressing the spice into the surface.

Heat olive oil in a wide, heavy kadai over medium-high heat. Place the chicken skin-side down (or smooth side down for skinless pieces) and sear without disturbing for 4–5 minutes until deeply golden. Flip and sear the other side for 3 minutes, then lift the chicken out and set aside — it will finish cooking with the rice.

Reduce the heat to medium. In the same kadai, add the onion and cook, stirring occasionally, for 5 minutes until soft and lightly golden. Add the garlic and capsicum strips and cook for another 3 minutes until the capsicum begins to soften.

Add the grated tomato and the remaining half teaspoon of paprika to the sofrito. Cook over medium flame, stirring now and then, for 7–8 minutes until the mixture thickens into a dark, jammy paste and oil begins to shimmer at the edges — this is the sofrito and it is the heart of the dish.

Add the rice and stir it through the sofrito for 1–2 minutes so every grain is coated. Pour in the hot stock and the saffron water, taste the liquid for salt, and stir once to level everything out. Do not stir again after this point.

Nestle the seared chicken pieces back into the rice, scatter the peas around them if using, and bring the pot to a vigorous boil. Once boiling steadily, reduce the heat to the lowest possible setting and cook uncovered for 18–20 minutes. The surface will look dry and pitted when the rice is ready — this is normal.

Remove from heat, cover tightly with a lid or foil, and rest for 5 minutes. Scatter fresh parsley on top and bring the kadai directly to the table. Serve with lemon wedges so each person can squeeze juice over their portion.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
critical_checks: Verify poultry doneness with a food thermometer where possible; do not rely on colour alone.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: rice → Sona masoori or kolam rice are ideal; avoid basmati as the long grain stays separate rather than absorbing stock (Short to medium-grain varieties give the correct sticky-yet-distinct texture); saffron → A quarter teaspoon of turmeric dissolved in the soaking water gives similar colour without the floral flavour; Kashmiri saffron from Indian spice shops works perfectly; stock → Maggi or Knorr chicken stock cube dissolved in 750 ml hot water; or plain water with a bay leaf and an extra half teaspoon of salt; olive-oil → Sunflower oil or any neutral refined oil; olive oil adds a mild fruitiness but is not essential to the final flavour; capsicum → Shimla mirch (same vegetable, different name) is universally available; the red variety adds sweetness and colour
indian_kitchen_adaptation: A wide flat-bottomed kadai is the best substitute for the traditional shallow paellera; a wider base means more surface area for the socarrat to form. Sona masoori or kolam rice from any South Indian grocery replaces Bomba rice well. Use Kashmiri red chilli powder instead of smoked paprika — it has the same mild, earthy quality. Stock cubes from Maggi or Knorr are widely available and convenient. Saffron is sold at Indian spice shops and paan stores; a small 1 g packet is enough for four to five batches.
